agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services. The CEO describes an automated system that adjusts delivery prices based on demand levels, with the dual goal of competitiveness and profitability. The description is high-level and intentionally vague, leaving many critical business details undefined.","bounded_context":"Pricing","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"DynaPricing","definition":"The product name for the real-time dynamic delivery pricing system"},{"term":"Demand","definition":"A measure of current delivery request volume relative to capacity — exact definition unspecified (geographic scope, time window, capacity metric all undefined)"},{"term":"Guardrails","definition":"Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme pricing — thresholds unspecified"},{"term":"Base Price","definition":"Implied but never stated: the reference price from which adjustments are made up or down"},{"term":"Price Adjustment","definition":"A change to delivery price driven by demand signals, applied in real time"}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery price must increase when demand is high relative to a baseline","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy encoding the core pricing strategy. A VP could change the demand-price relationship (e.g., cap surges, change the curve shape, add time delays). The direction of the correlation is policy, not physics.","testable_assertion":"Given demand above the high-demand threshold, the computed delivery price must be greater than the base price"},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Delivery price must decrease when demand is low relative to a baseline","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Business policy — management could decide to hold prices flat during low demand instead of reducing them. The inverse correlation is a strategic choice.","testable_assertion":"Given demand below the low-demand threshold, the computed delivery price must be less than the base price"},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Price adjustments must occur in real time (not batch or scheduled)","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy about system responsiveness. Management could decide to batch price updates every 5 minutes or hourly. 'Real time' is a preference, not a physical constraint.","testable_assertion":"When a demand signal changes, the price offered to the next customer must reflect the updated demand within the system's defined latency SLA"},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Pricing must be fully automated with no manual approval required per price change","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Operational policy. Management could add manual override or approval gates at any time, especially for edge cases. Automation level is a business decision.","testable_assertion":"A price adjustment triggered by demand change must be applied without any human approval step in the workflow"},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Price guardrails must exist to prevent prices from exceeding acceptable bounds","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Without guardrails, the system could produce prices that are negative, zero, absurdly high, or legally problematic. The EXISTENCE of guardrails is a system integrity constraint — a pricing system that can output unbounded values is corrupt. The specific thresholds are policy (BR-6, BR-7), but the constraint that bounds MUST exist is invariant.","testable_assertion":"The system must reject or clamp any computed price that falls outside configured minimum and maximum bounds"},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"A maximum price ceiling must be enforced (price must not exceed an upper bound)","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The specific ceiling value is a business policy that can be adjusted by leadership based on market conditions, regulation, or PR concerns.","testable_assertion":"No delivery price offered to a customer may exceed the configured maximum price ceiling"},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"A minimum price floor must be enforced (price must not fall below a lower bound)","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The specific floor value is policy. However, there is an implicit invariant that price must be greater than zero (BR-8).","testable_assertion":"No delivery price offered to a customer may fall below the configured minimum price floor"},{"id":"BR-8","rule":"Delivery price must always be a positive monetary value greater than zero","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A zero or negative delivery price is physically and financially nonsensical — it means the company pays the customer to deliver. This is a data integrity constraint, not a policy choice.","testable_assertion":"The final delivery price presented to any customer must be strictly greater than zero"},{"id":"BR-9","rule":"The system must maintain both competitiveness and profitability as dual objectives","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The balance between competitiveness and profitability is a strategic policy that shifts based on market conditions, growth stage, and leadership priorities. This is not a hard constraint but a guiding principle.","testable_assertion":"The pricing algorithm must be configurable to weight competitiveness vs profitability, and neither objective may be entirely zeroed out"}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Demand spikes to extreme levels (e.g., 10x normal) — how high can the price go?","expected_behavior":"Guardrail ceiling must cap the price. Exact ceiling is undefined in the spec."},{"scenario":"Demand drops to zero — no delivery requests at all","expected_behavior":"Price should hit the floor but remain positive. System must not divide by zero or produce undefined prices."},{"scenario":"Demand oscillates rapidly (e.g., every few seconds)","expected_behavior":"Price should adjust but avoid 'flapping' — customers seeing wildly different prices seconds apart. Rate-of-change damping may be needed (not specified)."},{"scenario":"Two customers request delivery at the same instant with demand changing between quote and confirmation","expected_behavior":"Unclear whether quoted price is locked at request time or can change before confirmation. Race condition in pricing."},{"scenario":"Geographic demand varies — high in one zone, low in another","expected_behavior":"Spec does not clarify whether demand is global, regional, or per-zone. A single global demand signal would misprice locally."},{"scenario":"Guardrail thresholds are misconfigured (e.g., min > max)","expected_behavior":"System must validate guardrail configuration and reject contradictory bounds at configuration time, not at pricing time."},{"scenario":"External event causes demand surge (e.g., natural disaster, major event)","expected_behavior":"Surge pricing during emergencies raises ethical/legal/PR risks. No policy defined for emergency demand scenarios."},{"scenario":"Cost of delivery exceeds the guardrail ceiling price","expected_behavior":"The company would deliver at a loss. The spec does not address cost-floor vs price-ceiling conflicts."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Dynamic pricing directly affects revenue and margin per delivery. Upside: capture surplus during high demand. Downside: mispriced guardrails could either leave money on the table or drive customers to competitors. Incorrect floor could cause sustained losses during low-demand periods.","operational":"Full automation reduces manual pricing overhead but introduces monitoring burden. System failures could silently misprice thousands of deliveries before detection. Requires robust observability.","user":"Customers may perceive dynamic pricing as unfair, especially during surges. Price transparency and communication strategy are critical. Frequent price changes may erode trust if not explained.","risk":"HIGH — Regulatory risk in jurisdictions with price-gouging laws. Reputational risk from surge pricing during emergencies. Technical risk from real-time system failures causing incorrect prices. Competitive risk if guardrails are set too conservatively.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Direct price impact on every delivery. Perception of fairness. May drive churn if surges feel exploitative."},{"group":"Couriers/Drivers","impact":"Higher demand pricing may attract more drivers during peak times (if they benefit from surge). Relationship between DynaPricing and courier compensation is undefined."},{"group":"Finance/Revenue Team","impact":"Revenue forecasting becomes more complex with variable pricing. Need new reporting on price distribution and margin analysis."},{"group":"Operations/Dispatch","impact":"Must integrate demand signals into pricing engine. Monitoring and alerting for anomalous pricing behavior."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"Must review for price-gouging regulations, consumer protection laws, and contractual obligations with enterprise customers."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will receive complaints about price variability. Need training and scripts to explain dynamic pricing."},{"group":"Sales/Enterprise Accounts","impact":"Enterprise customers with contracted rates may be exempt or affected. Relationship between DynaPricing and existing contracts is undefined."}]},"confidence":{"level":30,"reasoning":"The CEO's description is an informal all-hands soundbite, not a product specification. Almost every critical detail is undefined: demand measurement methodology, geographic scope, guardrail thresholds, price curve shape, quote locking behavior, cost-floor relationship, regulatory compliance approach, and courier compensation impact. The word 'probably' regarding guardrails is especially concerning — the most critical safety feature is not even confirmed. This is a vision statement, not a spec.","high_confidence_areas":["Price increases with high demand (explicitly stated)","Price decreases with low demand (explicitly stated)","System is automated (explicitly stated)","Some form of guardrails will exist (stated, though hedged with 'probably')"],"low_confidence_areas":["Definition and measurement of 'demand' (geographic scope, time window, capacity metric)","Specific guardrail thresholds (min/max price bounds)","Whether guardrails are even confirmed ('probably')","Price curve shape and sensitivity","Quote locking — is the price guaranteed once shown to a customer?","Relationship between delivery cost and price floor","Geographic granularity of demand signals","Impact on courier/driver compensation","Regulatory and legal compliance strategy","Enterprise/contract customer exemptions","Rate-of-change damping to prevent price flapping"],"assumptions":["A base price exists from which adjustments are calculated","Guardrails will indeed be implemented despite the 'probably' qualifier","'Real time' means near-real-time with some acceptable latency, not literally instantaneous","Demand is measurable via some quantifiable signal (request volume, queue depth, etc.)","Price must always be positive — the company never pays customers to accept delivery"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The CEO said 'probably' regarding guardrails. This is the single most critical safety feature of a dynamic pricing system. Without confirmed guardrails, the system could produce unbounded prices. Is the guardrail a confirmed requirement or still under discussion?","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Legal/Compliance","Finance","Engineering"],"options":["Treat guardrails as a hard requirement regardless of 'probably'","Treat guardrails as optional/deferred and proceed without them initially"],"recommendation":"Treat guardrails as a hard requirement. No responsible dynamic pricing system ships without price bounds. Escalate to CEO for confirmation."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"'Demand' is never defined. Is it per-zone, per-city, per-route, or global? What signals constitute demand — active requests, forecasted requests, courier availability ratio? Without a precise demand definition, the entire pricing model is ambiguous.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Product","Operations"],"options":["Define demand as request volume per geographic zone per time window","Define demand as courier-to-request ratio","Defer to data science team for demand model definition"],"recommendation":"Require Product to define demand precisely before engineering begins. The demand model is the foundation of the entire system."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No mention of price locking behavior. When a customer sees a quoted price, is it guaranteed through checkout? Or can it change between quote and confirmation? This is critical for customer trust and has direct financial impact.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Engineering","Customer Support"],"options":["Lock price at quote time for a defined window (e.g., 5 minutes)","Price is indicative only, confirmed at booking time","Lock price at checkout initiation"],"recommendation":"Lock price at quote time for a reasonable window. Customers who see one price and are charged another will churn and generate support tickets."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No mention of how DynaPricing interacts with existing pricing structures — enterprise contracts, promotional pricing, subscription plans, or loyalty discounts. Dynamic pricing layered on top of existing commitments could violate contractual obligations.","affected_stakeholders":["Sales","Enterprise Accounts","Legal"],"options":["DynaPricing applies only to on-demand retail customers","DynaPricing overrides all existing pricing","DynaPricing is additive (surcharge/discount on top of base)"],"recommendation":"Clarify scope immediately. Enterprise contract violations would be a serious legal and commercial risk."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Competitive but also profitable' is a tension, not a rule. These objectives conflict during high-cost, low-demand periods. No guidance on which objective takes priority, or how the tradeoff is managed.","affected_stakeholders":["Finance","Product","Executive Team"],"options":["Profitability as hard floor, competitiveness as optimization target","Competitiveness as primary, profitability as monitoring alert","Configurable weighting between the two objectives"],"recommendation":"Require Finance and Product to define a clear priority hierarchy or configurable weighting. Without this, engineering will make an arbitrary choice that may not align with business intent."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
